hints: Restrict to Y‚â•X and double; there |X‚àíY|=Y‚àíX.
Condition X+Y‚â•2(Y‚àíX) becomes Y‚â§3X.
Area of {X‚â§Y‚â§3X} in the unit square is 1/3; doubling gives 2/3.
solution: By symmetry consider $Y\ge X$ (then double). There $|X-Y|=Y-X$, and $X+Y\ge2(Y-X)\iff Y\le3X$. The area of $\{X\le Y\le 3X\}$ in the unit square is $\int_0^{1/3}2x\,dx+\int_{1/3}^1(1-x)\,dx=\tfrac19+\tfrac29=\tfrac13$. Doubling, $P=\tfrac23.$

hints: Only abc matters; note 10¬∑(abc) ‚â° (bca) mod 999 and 37 | 999.
So if 37 | abc then 37 also divides bca and cab automatically.
Count abc in 0..999 divisible by 37: ‚åä999/37‚åã+1 = 28.
solution: Since $x=37000+\overline{abc}$, the conditions are that $\overline{abc},\overline{bca},\overline{cab}$ are all divisible by $37$. Because $10\cdot\overline{abc}\equiv\overline{bca}\pmod{999}$ and $37\mid999$, divisibility of $\overline{abc}$ by $37$ forces the same for its cyclic shifts. So we just count $\overline{abc}\in\{0,\dots,999\}$ divisible by $37$: $\lfloor999/37\rfloor+1=27+1=28.$

hints: Only the units digit of m matters: {1,3,5,7,9}. n ranges over 20 consecutive years.
Units-1 always works; 5 never; 3 and 7 need n‚â°0 mod4 (5/20 each); 9 needs n even (1/2).
solution:
Only the units digit of $m$ matters: $\{1,3,5,7,9\}$, each with probability $\tfrac15$; $n$ is uniform over $20$ consecutive integers. Units digit $1$: $m^n$ always ends in $1$. Units $5$: never. Units $3$ or $7$: ends in $1$ iff $n\equiv0\pmod4$ ($5/20=\tfrac14$). Units $9$: iff $n$ even ($\tfrac12$). So
$$P=\tfrac15\Big(1+0+\tfrac14+\tfrac14+\tfrac12\Big)=\tfrac15\cdot2=\tfrac25.$$

hints: Arrange S as a 2√ó11 grid (rows 1‚Äì11 and 12‚Äì22); difference 1 = horizontal, 11 = vertical. Count domino tilings.
Plain 2√ó11 tilings = Fibonacci F(12)=144. But the pair {11,12} also differs by 1 (not a grid neighbor) ‚Äî an extra option.
Using {11,12} forces the rest uniquely (+1), so total = 144+1 = 145.
solution: Place $S$ in a $2\times11$ grid (top row $1..11$, bottom $12..22$). Pairs differing by $1$ are horizontal neighbors and by $11$ are vertical neighbors, so most partitions are domino tilings of the grid: $F(12)=144$. The pair $\{11,12\}$ also differs by $1$ but is not a grid edge ‚Äî an extra allowed pairing. Using it removes $11,12$ and forces every remaining pair uniquely, adding exactly $1$ partition. Total $=144+1=145.$

hints: Ignore rolls that aren't 5 or 6; among those, 5 and 6 are equally likely.
P(exactly k fives before the first six)=(1/2)^k¬∑(1/2).
solution: Only $5$s and $6$s matter, and conditioned on a relevant roll each is equally likely. The number of $5$s before the first $6$ is geometric: $P(\text{exactly }k)=\big(\tfrac12\big)^k\cdot\tfrac12$. For $k=4$, $P=\big(\tfrac12\big)^5=\tfrac1{32}.$

hints: Given sin(X)=s, X could be x or œÄ‚àíx, symmetric about œÄ/2.
These two preimages are equally likely under the uniform law, so E[X | sin X] = œÄ/2.
cos(œÄ/2)=0.
solution: For $X\sim U[0,\pi]$, a value $\sin(X)=s$ corresponds to two equally likely points $x$ and $\pi-x$, symmetric about $\tfrac\pi2$. Hence $\mathbb{E}[X\mid\sin X]=\tfrac{x+(\pi-x)}2=\tfrac\pi2$ for every value, and $\cos\big(\tfrac\pi2\big)=0.$

hints: The call is ITM (S‚ÇÄ=25>K=23); as expiry nears its delta drifts toward 1, while your static hedge stays at 0.74.
Approximate delta linearly from 0.74 (now) to 1 (expiry, 4h). At 2h: 0.74+¬Ω(1‚àí0.74)=0.87.
Portfolio delta = 0.87 ‚àí 0.74 = 0.13.
solution: The call is in-the-money, so as expiry approaches its delta drifts toward $1$; the hedge (short $0.74$ shares) is static. Approximating the delta linearly from $0.74$ now to $1$ at the $4$-hour expiry, after $2$ hours it is $0.74+\tfrac12(1-0.74)=0.87$. The portfolio delta is $0.87-0.74=0.13.$

hints: max{0,x}‚â•0 and min{0,y}‚â§0, so they're equal only if both are 0.
solution: $\max\{0,x\}\ge0$ and $\min\{0,y\}\le0$, so equality requires both to be $0$: $x\le0$ and $y\ge0$. Each of $x\le0$ and $y\ge0$ has $11$ of $21$ values, so $P=\big(\tfrac{11}{21}\big)^2=\tfrac{121}{441}.$

hints: Put delta = call delta ‚àí 1 = 0.25 ‚àí 1 = ‚àí0.75.
Selling 100 puts gives portfolio delta ‚àí100¬∑(‚àí0.75)=+75.
Hedge to 0 by selling 75 units ‚áí ‚àí75.
solution: By put‚Äìcall parity the put's delta is $0.25-1=-0.75$. Selling $100$ puts gives a position delta of $-100\times(-0.75)=+75$. To neutralize it you sell $75$ units of the underlying, i.e. $-75.$

hints: At most one of the 6 pieces can exceed 1/2; compute P(some piece > 1/2).
For 5 uniform cuts (6 spacings), P(some spacing > t) = C(6,1)(1‚àít)^5 for t=1/2 (higher terms vanish).
solution: At most one piece can exceed $\tfrac12$. For $5$ uniform cuts (giving $6$ spacings), the probability some spacing exceeds $t$ is $\binom{6}{1}(1-t)^5$ (the inclusion‚Äìexclusion terms with $1-2t\le0$ vanish at $t=\tfrac12$). So $P(\text{some piece}>\tfrac12)=6\cdot(\tfrac12)^5=\tfrac{3}{16}$, and $P(\text{none})=1-\tfrac3{16}=\tfrac{13}{16}.$

hints: Arrivals N ~ Poisson(7); total service time T = (1/6)¬∑N hours (10 min each).
solution: Arrivals $N\sim\mathrm{Poisson}(7)$, and each takes $\tfrac16$ hour, so total time $T=\tfrac16N$. Then $\mathbb{E}[T]=\tfrac16\cdot7=\tfrac76$ and $\mathrm{Var}(T)=\tfrac1{36}\cdot7=\tfrac7{36}$ (Poisson mean=variance). Their sum is $\tfrac76+\tfrac7{36}=\tfrac{42+7}{36}=\tfrac{49}{36}.$

hints: Apply It√¥ to X=ln(S) with dS=ŒºS dt+œÉS dW (Œº=3, œÉ=4).
dX = (Œº ‚àí œÉ¬≤/2)dt + œÉ dW = (3 ‚àí 8)dt + 4dW.
solution: By It√¥'s lemma for $X=\ln S$ with $dS=3S\,dt+4S\,dW$: $dX=\big(3-\tfrac12\cdot4^2\big)dt+4\,dW=-5\,dt+4\,dW$. So $a=-5,\,b=4$ and $a^2+b^2=25+16=41.$
